questions.question: What is the main benefit of storing Temporal connection settings in a configuration file or environment variables, rather than writing them directly in your application code?

choices.text: It makes your application run faster by caching the connection details
It lets you switch between environments like development and production without changing your code
It automatically creates a backup of your connection settings
It prevents other developers from seeing your server address

questions.explanation: When connection settings (like server address, namespace, and API keys) are kept separate from your code — in a config file or environment variables — you can point your app at a different environment simply by changing those settings. No code edits are needed. This separation is a common best practice called 'decoupling configuration from code,' and it makes managing multiple environments (development, staging, production) much easier.

questions.question: When both an environment variable and a TOML configuration file define the same setting, which one does Temporal use?

choices.text: The TOML file, because it is more structured and explicit
Whichever setting was created most recently
The environment variable, because it always takes highest priority
Temporal will throw an error because two sources conflict

questions.explanation: Temporal uses a clear order of priority: environment variables always win over anything written in a TOML config file. This design is intentional — environment variables are commonly used to inject secrets or override settings in dynamic environments (like containers or CI/CD pipelines) without touching files. Think of environment variables as a 'last word' override that sits on top of everything else.

questions.question: In a Temporal TOML configuration file, what is the purpose of having multiple 'profiles'?

choices.text: To run multiple Temporal Clients at the same time from one application
To store separate connection settings for different environments in a single file
To define different retry policies for different types of work
To control which programming languages can connect to Temporal

questions.explanation: A profile is simply a named block of settings inside your TOML file. For example, you might have a 'default' profile pointing to your local machine for development, and a 'prod' profile pointing to Temporal Cloud. By switching profiles, you change which set of connection details your app uses — all without maintaining separate files. If no profile is specified, Temporal automatically uses the one named 'default'.

questions.question: You need to connect a Temporal client in Go using the default environment configuration profile. Which function call correctly loads the default profile and environment variables?

choices.text: client.Dial(envconfig.LoadDefaultProfile())
client.Dial(envconfig.LoadClientOptions(envconfig.LoadClientOptionsRequest{ConfigFileProfile: "default"}))
client.Dial(envconfig.MustLoadDefaultClientOptions())
envconfig.Connect("default")

questions.explanation: In Go, the correct function is envconfig.MustLoadDefaultClientOptions() from the go.temporal.io/sdk/contrib/envconfig package. This function loads the 'default' profile from the standard TOML config file location and merges in any environment variables, returning ClientOptions ready to pass to client.Dial(). You'd use this pattern to avoid hardcoding connection settings and support multiple deployment environments cleanly.

questions.question: A developer wants to use the Temporal CLI to switch between a local development environment and a Temporal Cloud production environment without editing the TOML config file each time. What is the correct approach?

choices.text: Create separate temporal.toml files in different directories and switch between them using TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE
Define named profiles in a single temporal.toml file and use the --profile flag with CLI commands (e.g., temporal --profile prod workflow list)
Use the TEMPORAL_NAMESPACE environment variable to switch between environments at runtime
Run temporal config reload before each command to switch environments

questions.explanation: Temporal supports named configuration profiles within a single temporal.toml file. You can define a 'default' profile for local development and a 'prod' profile for Temporal Cloud, then use the --profile flag (e.g., temporal --profile prod workflow list) to select which profile the CLI uses. This is the recommended approach for managing multiple environments without code or file changes. The TEMPORAL_PROFILE environment variable can also be used to set the active profile.

questions.question: When using the Activity Retry simulator, which combination of timeout settings would cause an Activity to fail permanently if a single attempt takes longer than 500ms, even if the Retry Policy allows more retries?

choices.text: Set scheduleToCloseTimeout to 5000ms and maximumAttempts to 3
Set startToCloseTimeout to 500ms — each attempt will fail if it exceeds 500ms, eventually exhausting retries
Set scheduleToStartTimeout to 500ms — this limits individual execution time per attempt
Set backoffCoefficient to 1 so retries happen immediately without delay

questions.explanation: The startToCloseTimeout defines the maximum time a single Activity Task Execution (one attempt) can take from start to finish. If an attempt exceeds this timeout, that attempt fails and Temporal will retry according to the Retry Policy. If all retries are exhausted with each attempt timing out at 500ms, the Activity Execution fails permanently. The scheduleToStartTimeout limits queue wait time, not execution time, and backoffCoefficient controls retry delay intervals, not attempt duration.

questions.question: A platform team maintains a single temporal.toml file shared across dev, staging, and production deployments. They have profiles named 'default', 'staging', and 'prod'. When a worker process starts without any profile-related environment variables set, which profile does the Temporal SDK load, and what happens if that profile doesn't exist in the file?

choices.text: The SDK loads the first profile defined in the file alphabetically, and gracefully falls back to environment variables if it doesn't exist.
The SDK loads the 'default' profile, and if that profile doesn't exist in the file, the application returns an error.
The SDK loads all profiles and merges them, with later profiles overriding earlier ones for any conflicting keys.
The SDK skips the TOML file entirely and falls back to connecting to localhost:7233 as a built-in default.

questions.explanation: When no profile is specified via the TEMPORAL_PROFILE environment variable or SDK load options, Temporal SDKs load the 'default' profile. Critically, if you request a profile that doesn't exist in the TOML file—including 'default'—the application returns an error rather than silently using fallback values. This is an important production safeguard: it prevents misconfigured deployments from silently connecting to the wrong environment. A common mistake teams make is deploying to a new environment without adding the corresponding profile to their TOML file, expecting Temporal to 'figure it out.' Instead, you get a clear error at startup, which is actually the right behavior—failing fast on misconfiguration is better than silently connecting to the wrong Temporal cluster.

questions.question: Your team uses Temporal's environment configuration system and needs to deploy to a new Kubernetes cluster. An engineer writes a Helm chart that sets TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE to point to a mounted ConfigMap. Another engineer argues this is unnecessary since they can just put the TOML file at the default OS path inside the container. A third engineer says you should use programmatic overrides after loading the profile instead. Which approach correctly describes how Temporal resolves the config file location?

choices.text: Temporal always requires TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE to be set explicitly; there is no default OS path lookup, so the ConfigMap mount approach is mandatory.

Temporal checks TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE first, then falls back to OS-specific default paths like ~/.config/temporalio/temporal.toml on Linux—so both the env var and the default path approaches are valid, but TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE takes precedence.
Temporal always reads from the OS default path first and only checks TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE if the default file is missing.
Temporal ignores file paths entirely in containerized environments and only accepts environment variables for configuration.

questions.explanation: Temporal resolves the config file location by first checking the TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE environment variable, and only if that's not set does it look at the OS-specific default path (~/.config/temporalio/temporal.toml on Linux, $HOME/Library/Application Support/temporalio/temporal.toml on macOS, %AppData%\temporalio\temporal.toml on Windows). This means both approaches work: you can mount a ConfigMap at the default Linux path, or explicitly point to any path via TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE. The TEMPORAL_CONFIG_FILE approach is more explicit and portable across environments, which is why it's preferred in Kubernetes. The programmatic override approach (third engineer) is also valid—the SDKs let you load a profile and then modify specific fields before connecting, which is useful when a profile has most settings correct but one value needs to be dynamically computed at runtime.

questions.question: A team is debugging why their Activity Execution keeps failing. Using the Activity Retry Simulator, they configure a scenario where the HTTP API takes 100ms to respond and has a 50% success rate. They set startToCloseTimeout to 10000ms, initialInterval to 1000ms, backoffCoefficient to 2, and maximumAttempts to 3. The Activity fails on all 3 attempts. Which setting is most likely causing the problem, and what value reveals that the issue is NOT about individual attempt timeouts?

choices.text: The startToCloseTimeout is too low at 10000ms; it should be at least 60000ms for a 100ms API call to have enough buffer for retries.
The backoffCoefficient is too high; with exponential backoff, attempts are spaced too far apart and the scheduleToCloseTimeout expires before all retries complete.

The startToCloseTimeout of 10000ms is sufficient per attempt (the 100ms API call is well under it), so individual attempts aren't timing out—the issue is the 50% success rate combined with maximumAttempts of 3 means there's only a 12.5% chance all 3 attempts fail, suggesting this is expected probabilistic behavior rather than a configuration error.
The initialInterval is set too high at 1000ms; the Task Time in Queue adds latency that causes the scheduleToStartTimeout to fire before each retry.

questions.explanation: The startToCloseTimeout of 10000ms is far above the 100ms API response time, so individual attempts are not timing out. With a 50% success rate and maximumAttempts of 3, the probability that all 3 attempts fail is 0.5^3 = 12.5%. This means in roughly 1 in 8 runs, you'd expect all attempts to fail—not because of misconfiguration, but because of the probabilistic nature of the success rate. This is a crucial lesson when using the Activity Retry Simulator: before assuming a timeout or retry policy is wrong, verify whether the observed failure rate matches the expected probability. The simulator is designed to help you understand this interplay between success rates, timeouts, and retry counts. A common engineering mistake is to keep increasing maximumAttempts without realizing the real fix is addressing why the underlying API has a 50% failure rate in the first place.
